studies.risk_class: Risk Class IIa according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Outcomes are collected at baseline, 12, and 24 weeks post-randomization. The waitlist control group receives the intervention after 6 months (24 weeks).
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group receives digital CBT-I (somnio) + Care as Usual (CAU). Control group is a waitlist receiving CAU only, with access to somnio after 6 months (24 weeks). 1:1 randomization.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Group 1 receives access to somnio (mementor DE GmbH), a digital health application delivering cognitive-behavioral psychotherapy for insomnia, in addition to CAU. Group 2 (waitlist control) receives CAU and waits 24 weeks before receiving access to somnio.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Depression severity (PHQ-9) at 12 weeks. Secondary outcomes (at 12 & 24 weeks): Insomnia severity (ISI), Daytime sleepiness (ESS), Fatigue (FSS), Quality of life (WHQOL_BREF), Emotional state (PANAS), Sleep continuity (sleep diary at 12 weeks), Self-efficacy (PCS), Emotion regulation (RSQ). Baseline screening included diagnostic interview and Beck Depression Inventory (BDI).

studies.risk_class: Risk Class IIa according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes, at 16 weeks post-randomization (8 weeks after the primary endpoint)
studies.duration_additional_info: The intervention duration is 8 weeks. Outcomes are collected at baseline, 8 weeks, and 16 weeks post-randomization.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention (digital cognitive behavioural therapy) = 29; Control (digital sleep monitoring) = 27
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Group 1 (Intervention) received access to 'somnio', a digital health application delivering cognitive-behavioral therapy for insomnia, including a digital sleep diary, for 8 weeks. Group 2 (Control) received access to 'malio', a digital health application enabling daily sleep tracking via a digital sleep diary, for 8 weeks. Both groups wore an activity tracker on their wrist for the 8-week duration.
studies.collected_data: Primary Outcome: Insomnia severity measured by the Insomnia Severity Index (ISI) at 8 & 16 weeks post-randomization. Secondary Outcomes: Sleep continuity (measured with a sleep diary and actigraphy continuously for 8 weeks); Dysfunctional beliefs about sleep (DBAS; 8 & 16 weeks); Pre-Sleep Arousal (PSAS; 8 & 16 weeks); Daytime well-being (WHO-5; 8 & 16 weeks); Quality of life (WHQOL_BREF; 8 & 16 weeks); Symptoms of anxiety and depression (HADS; 8 & 16 weeks).
studies.key_outcomes_findings: At 8- and 16-weeks post-randomisation, large treatment effects (d = 0.87–1.08) indicated robust reductions (-3.70 and -2.97, respectively; p ≤ 0.003) in insomnia severity in the digital cognitive behavioural therapy arm, relative to digital sleep monitoring. Treatment effects in favour of digital cognitive behavioural therapy were also found for self-reported and actigraphy-derived sleep continuity variables, indicating that sleep improved throughout the 8-week intervention period.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class IIa according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Symptoms are measured prior to the intervention as well as 10 weeks and six months after inclusion in the study.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: Cognitive-behavioral therapy for insomnia via the 'Somnio' app in addition to treatment-as-usual. Control: Filling in of sleep-diary in addition to treatment-as-usual.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 receives cognitive-behavioral therapy for insomnia delivered via the 'Somnio' app as an add-on to treatment-as-usual in the trauma clinic. Arm 2 receives a sleep-diary to fill in as an add-on to treatment-as-usual in the trauma clinic.
studies.collected_data: Posttraumatic symptoms (PCL-5, PTCI), sleep quality (sleep diaries, PSQI), anxiety (GAD-7), depression (QIDS-SR16), and life quality (WHODAS 2.0).

studies.risk_class: Risk Class IIa according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: The intervention consists of 11 modules completed over a period of 8 weeks.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group: Digital cognitive behavioural therapy for insomnia (dCBT-I); Control group: Waitlist control group.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention arm receives the somnio therapy programme consisting of 11 modules (approx. 3.5 hours in total) completed over 8 weeks. Control arm is a waitlist control group.
studies.collected_data: ISI Insomnia Severity Index, German Pain Questionnaire (DSF), Multidimensional Pain Inventory (MPI), Epworth Sleepiness Scale (ESS), Fatigue Severity Scale (FSS), Opinions About Sleep Questionnaire (DBAS), WHO-5 Well-Being Index, WHO Quality of Life (WHOQOL_BREF), Basic Psychological Need Satisfaction and Frustration Scale (BPNSFS), Mannheim Dream Questionnaire (MADRE), Nightmare Distress Questionnaire (NDQ), General Depression Scale (ADS-K), State-Trait Anxiety Inventory (STAI), Questionnaire for Analysis of Motivational Schemas (FAMOS), Incongruence Questionnaire (K-INK).

studies.risk_class: Risk Class IIa according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: The digital CBT-I intervention consists of 11 modules completed over a period of 6-8 weeks.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The intervention group received 8 weeks of digital cognitive-behavioral therapy for insomnia (dCBT-I) plus care-as-usual (CAU). The therapy consists of 11 modules (approx. 20 mins each, 3.5 hours total). The control group was placed on a waitlist plus care-as-usual (CAU).
studies.collected_data: Insomnia Severity Index (ISI), DBAS-16 (dysfunctional beliefs and attitudes about sleep), FSS (Fatigue Severity Index), ESS (Epworth Sleepiness Scale), CES-D (Center for Epidemiologic Studies Depression Scale, short version), STAI-T (State-Trait Anxiety Inventory), WHOQOL_BREF (WHO Quality of Life Questionnaire, brief version), WHO-5 (WHO-Five Well-Being Index), MADRE (Mannheim Dream Questionnaire), DTI (Düsseldorf Dream Inventory), NDQ (Nightmare Distress Questionnaire), CAN (Cognitive Appraisal of Nightmares).
studies.key_outcomes_findings: At posttreatment (8 weeks), dCBT-I was associated with a large reduction in the ISI (adjusted difference = -7.60) compared to the waitlist control (d = -2.08). Clinical improvement was reflected in responder and remission rates. Treatment effects were also observed for daytime functioning, quality of life, symptoms of depression and anxiety (ds = 0.26-1.02), and at long-term follow-up for the intervention group (ds = 0.18-1.65). No effects were found for dream and nightmare frequency.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class IIa according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Assessments took place at baseline, 12- and 24-weeks post randomization.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received fully automated digital cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia (dCBT-I) added to care-as-usual (CAU), delivered via a mobile app or web platform consisting of 10 sequential core modules. Control group was a waiting list control (WLC).
studies.collected_data: Severity of depressive symptoms (Patient Health Questionnaire-9; PHQ-9), insomnia severity, daytime sleepiness, fatigue, well-being, and mechanistic effect measures.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Large treatment effects at 12- and 24 weeks showed significant reductions in depressive symptoms (-3.34 and -2.83; ps <0.001; ds = 0.66-0.78) in the dCBT-I group. Treatment effects in favor of dCBT-I were also found for insomnia severity (ds = 1.46-1.94) and most secondary outcomes (ds = 0.33-1.14). The study demonstrates that digital dCBT-I can be effective for individuals with depression and comorbid insomnia, with stable effects.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class IIa according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Assessments took place at baseline, 8 and 24 weeks post-randomization.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Participants were randomized (1:1) to either digital cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia (dCBT-I) added to regular treatment of chronic pain, or a waitlist control (WLC) condition.
studies.collected_data: Insomnia severity (Insomnia Severity Index [ISI]), sleep- and pain-related symptoms, measures of well-being and dreams, daytime sleepiness, fatigue, and dysfunctional beliefs and attitudes about sleep.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: The dCBT-I group showed large improvements in the severity of insomnia compared to the WLC both after 8 weeks (-4.36, p < 0.001; d = -1.18) and 24 weeks (-4.88, p < 0.001; d = -1.32). Pain-related impairments and life control also improved, favoring dCBT-I (ds = 0.22-0.35). Moderate-to-large treatment effects were also observed for secondary outcomes, including daytime sleepiness, fatigue, and dysfunctional beliefs and attitudes about sleep (ds = 0.47-1.12).

studies.risk_class: Risk Class IIa according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: 8 weeks of intervention; assessments at baseline, 8- and 16-weeks post-randomisation.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: digital cognitive behavioural therapy (n = 29) and digital sleep monitoring (n = 27)
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Participants were randomly allocated (1:1) to 8 weeks of digital cognitive behavioural therapy or 8 weeks of digital sleep monitoring (control application).
studies.collected_data: Insomnia severity; self-reported and actigraphy-derived sleep continuity variables via application-integrated sleep diaries and actigraphy.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: At 8- and 16-weeks post-randomisation, large treatment effects (d = 0.87-1.08) indicated robust reductions (-3.70 and -2.97, respectively; p ≤ 0.003) in insomnia severity in the digital cognitive behavioural therapy arm relative to digital sleep monitoring. Treatment effects in favour of digital cognitive behavioural therapy were also found for self-reported and actigraphy-derived sleep continuity variables.
